headline: A Global Movement Builds Bridges for Wildlife
meta_description: Wildlife crossings are becoming mainstream infrastructure worldwide, with overwhelming scientific evidence showing they protect endangered animals and reduce c…
summary_short: From Brazil to Colorado, wildlife crossings are expanding globally, backed by strong science and rare bipartisan support.
body:
When Brazil built its first wildlife bridge across the busy BR-101 highway, it represented more than concrete and steel. Partners including the Golden Lion Tamarin Association began reforesting corridors to connect protected areas—transforming infrastructure into a lifeline for fragmented habitats.

This project exemplifies a quiet revolution. Journalist Ben Goldfarb, author of *Crossings*, has documented how wildlife crossings are gaining momentum worldwide, from North America to Europe to South America. The reason? Evidence.

"At this point, the evidence that wildlife crossing structures are effective is just overwhelming," Goldfarb explains. Scientists have documented that animals of all shapes and sizes successfully use these structures, reducing collisions, protecting threatened species, and improving genetic diversity.

What makes this movement remarkable is its political consensus. A bill is currently before the U.S. Congress to make the Wildlife Crossings Pilot Program permanent. Public surveys show overwhelming support. Environmental coordinator Andy Holland observed that "the whole world is open to wildlife now."

Goldfarb calls highway overpasses "billboards for connectivity"—visual reminders that we share landscapes with other species. In an era of polarization, wildlife crossings offer something rare: infrastructure that helps both animals and humans, uniting people across political lines around a simple truth: coexistence is possible.
